summary:
The HOX gene influences a variety of anatomical structures including bones, limbs, genitourinary organs, and neural tissues; it plays crucial roles not only in conditions such as VACTERL association but also directly impacts craniofacial formation contributing significantly towards disorders involving midline defects due to its essential function in early embryo segmentation processes. Given these findings, specific congenital issues related to improper head shape or facial features would be most indicative of altered HOX expression patterns compared solely focusing on isolated hand/foot/genitalia changes alone. To pinpoint exactly one condition from options given without more precise details about them isn't feasible here though based broadly speaking any anomaly linked strongly back into forebrain/cranium growth could likely involve misregulated Hox signaling pathways at some level making Holoprosencephaly highly plausible choice among listed possibilities despite lacking exact match between all stated symptoms across different sources consulted thus far regarding known associations linking particular mutations within said family members together consistently over time throughout various studies conducted globally investigating human genetics research fields today.
To summarize:

The HOX gene affects diverse areas such as skeleton, urogenitals, and CNS via critical functions in body plan establishment; therefore, malformations impacting overall skull/facial structure are strong indicators of HOX-related disruption. Key examples include spinal/bone deformities seen in VACTERL & Chiari syndromes alongside potential contributions toward holoprosencephaly through impaired frontonasal axis development driven primarily by aberrant HOX activities during gestation periods when proper morphogenesis relies heavily upon coordinated spatial cues orchestrated centrally around conserved genetic blueprints encoded
